chapter_info.subject: Social Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Universal Franchise and India's Electoral System

questions.question: What is the meaning of 'Universal Adult Franchise'?
questions.answer: Universal adult franchise means that every adult citizen of the country has the right to vote, regardless of their gender, caste, religion, education, or wealth.
questions.solution: This principle is the cornerstone of democracy, ensuring that every citizen has an equal voice. In India, any citizen aged 18 or above is eligible to vote.

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution enshrines the principle of universal adult franchise?
questions.answer: Article 326 of the Indian Constitution enshrines this principle.
questions.solution: Article 326 states that elections to the Lok Sabha and the Legislative Assemblies of every State shall be on the basis of adult suffrage.

questions.question: In which year was the Election Commission of India (ECI) established?
questions.answer: The Election Commission of India was established in 1950.
questions.solution: It was established as an independent constitutional body just before the first general elections held in 1951-52.

questions.question: What are the two synonymous terms for 'Franchise' often used in the context of voting?
questions.answer: The two synonymous terms are 'Suffrage' and 'Franchise'.
questions.solution: Both terms refer to the right to vote granted by a constitution or government to its citizens.

questions.question: What was the minimum voting age in India before it was changed to 18 in 1988?
questions.answer: The minimum voting age was 21 years.
questions.solution: The age was lowered to 18 in 1988 to increase youth participation in the democratic process.

questions.question: Define a 'Constituency'.
questions.answer: A constituency is a specific geographical area whose voters elect a representative to a legislative body like the Lok Sabha or Vidhan Sabha.
questions.solution: India is divided into 543 parliamentary constituencies for the Lok Sabha elections.

questions.question: What does 'NOTA' stand for in an electoral system?
questions.answer: NOTA stands for 'None of the Above'.
questions.solution: It is an option on the EVM that allows a voter to register their disapproval if they are not satisfied with any of the candidates contesting.

questions.question: Who was the first voter in India's general elections of 1951?
questions.answer: Shyam Saran Negi was the first voter.
questions.solution: He was a school teacher from Himachal Pradesh and famously continued to cast his vote until the age of over 100.

questions.question: Name the independent body responsible for conducting local body elections like Gram Panchayats.
questions.answer: The State Election Commission is responsible for local body elections.
questions.solution: While the ECI manages national and state elections, each state has its own commission for village and city-level governance bodies.

questions.question: How many seats are reserved for Scheduled Castes (SC) and Scheduled Tribes (ST) in the Lok Sabha?
questions.answer: 84 seats are reserved for SC and 47 seats are reserved for ST.
questions.solution: Reservations ensure that members of these historically marginalized communities are represented in the national parliament.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha in terms of election process?
questions.answer: Lok Sabha members are directly elected by the people, whereas Rajya Sabha members are indirectly elected by members of state legislative assemblies (MLAs).
questions.solution: Lok Sabha represents the people of India directly, while the Rajya Sabha represents the states and union territories in the federal structure.

questions.question: Explain the role of the 'Returning Officer' (RO) in an election.
questions.answer: The Returning Officer is responsible for conducting the election in a specific constituency.
questions.solution: The RO manages the nomination process, the actual polling, and the counting of votes for that particular area.

questions.question: What is the 'First-Past-the-Post' system used in India?
questions.answer: It is an electoral system where the candidate who receives the most votes in a constituency wins, even if they do not secure a majority (more than 50%) of the total votes.
questions.solution: As seen in the case of Gurmat in the story, she won with 12 votes out of 33 because she had more than Ahmed and Ravi.

questions.question: Why is the Rajya Sabha called a 'Permanent House'?
questions.answer: The Rajya Sabha is never dissolved; instead, one-third of its members retire every two years and new members are elected for a six-year term.
questions.solution: This ensures continuity in the upper house, unlike the Lok Sabha which has a fixed term of five years and is dissolved before every general election.

questions.question: What are the two main purposes of the PM Poshan (formerly Mid-Day Meal) scheme mentioned in the context of schooling?
questions.answer: The two purposes are: (1) To improve the nutritional intake of children, and (2) To improve school enrollment and reduce dropouts among poor children.
questions.solution: By providing a daily meal, the scheme encourages parents to send their children to school regularly, thus fulfilling the right to education.

questions.question: Identify the four polling officers' duties on the day of voting at a polling station.
questions.answer: 1st: Checks ID; 2nd: Inks finger and gives chit; 3rd: Verifies chit/finger; 4th: VVPAT/EVM operation.
questions.solution: This multi-step verification process ensures that every vote is cast by an eligible, registered voter safely and fairly.

questions.question: How does technology like VVPAT improve the trust in Electronic Voting Machines (EVMs)?
questions.answer: VVPAT produces a physical paper slip which the voter can see to verify their choice, and which acts as a backup for re-counting in case of disputes.
questions.solution: It provides a 'paper audit trail' that confirms the electronic vote was recorded correctly for the intended candidate.

questions.question: Describe the composition of the Election Commission of India (ECI).
questions.answer: The ECI consists of a Chief Election Commissioner (CEC) and two Election Commissioners.
questions.solution: This collective leadership was established to ensure balanced and fair decision-making in overseeing the world's largest democratic elections.

questions.question: What is the 'Model Code of Conduct' (MCC) and when does it come into effect?
questions.answer: The MCC is a set of guidelines for candidates and political parties to ensure fair elections; it comes into effect as soon as the ECI announces the election schedule.
questions.solution: It prevents the ruling party from misusing govt resources and prohibits candidates from using hate speech or bribery to influence voters.

questions.question: What is a 'Coalition Government'?
questions.answer: A coalition government is formed when two or more political parties work together to secure a majority in the Lok Sabha or Legislative Assembly.
questions.solution: If no single party wins more than half the seats, parties with similar goals join forces to form a government and choose a leader.

questions.question: Compare India's early adoption of universal franchise with the evolution of voting rights in Switzerland.
questions.answer: India granted universal adult franchise to all citizens (including women) immediately in 1950, whereas Switzerland, a much older democracy, did not grant women the right to vote until 1971.
questions.solution: This highlights the 'abundant faith in the common man' and the deeply progressive values of India's Constitution makers compared to many Western nations.

questions.question: Evaluate the significance of the 10th-century Uttaramerur inscriptions in the context of democratic traditions in India.
questions.answer: The inscriptions describe a transparent process where representatives were selected fairly using palm leaves in a pot, showing that organized selection of leaders existed in India long ago.
questions.solution: This historical evidence refutes the idea that democracy is a purely Western import and shows ancient roots of accountability and public selection in India.

questions.question: Why is the T.N. Seshan era remembered as a turning point for Indian elections?
questions.answer: Seshan enforced strict reforms like voter IDs, restrictions on proxy voting, and rigorous monitoring of election spending and the Model Code of Conduct.
questions.solution: His proactive leadership transformed the ECI into a fearless institution, making elections significantly more transparent and fair than before.

questions.question: Analyze why urban areas often show lower voter turnout compared to rural areas.
questions.answer: This trend, often called 'Urban Apathy', is due to factors like mobility of people, lack of local connection, or a sense that individual votes don't change outcomes.
questions.solution: Low turnout (e.g., 34% not voting in 2024) is a challenge for democracy because it reduces the representative nature of the elected government.

questions.question: Explain the 'Electoral College' system for electing the President of India.
questions.answer: The President is elected not by the common people directly, but by a body (Electoral College) consisting of elected MPs from both houses of Parliament and elected MLAs from all states.
questions.solution: This indirect system ensures that the President represents the collective will of the people as expressed through their directly elected national and state representatives.

questions.question: How does the 'Single Transferable Vote' (STV) system work in Rajya Sabha elections?
questions.answer: It is a system where voters rank candidates by preference. If their first choice is eliminated, their vote is transferred to their next choice, ensuring fair representation.
questions.solution: STV is used in indirect elections to ensure that smaller states and diverse political groups have a voice in the Council of States (Rajya Sabha).

questions.question: Why are certain constituencies 'reserved' for SC and ST candidates?
questions.answer: Reservation ensures that communities that have historically faced social discrimination are given a guaranteed space in the decision-making process of the country.
questions.solution: By ensuring their presence in the Lok Sabha (84 SC, 47 ST), the democratic system aims to bridge social barriers and promote inclusive nation-building.

questions.question: What is meant by the 'Spirit of the Age' as used in the context of local body elections?
questions.answer: It refers to the massive scale of grassroots democracy in India, with 3.1 million elected local representatives (many of whom are women).
questions.solution: This scale shows that democracy is not just about the national capital but reaches every village and city, making it a truly living and breathing system.

questions.question: Discuss the impact of using government resources for campaigning under the lens of 'Level Playing Field'.
questions.answer: Using govt assets (vehicles, ads) gives the ruling party an unfair advantage over the opposition, violating the requirement for a fair and equal contest.
questions.solution: The Model Code of Conduct strictly prohibits this to ensure that all candidates, whether in power or not, compete as equals during the election.

questions.question: What was the literacy rate in India in 1947 when universal franchise was first debated?
questions.answer: The literacy rate was about 14% (8% among women).
questions.solution: Despite this low rate, Indian leaders boldly chose universal franchise, trusting the political wisdom of the common man.

questions.question: What is the difference between an 'MP' and an 'MLA'?
questions.answer: An MP (Member of Parliament) represents a constituency at the national level, while an MLA (Member of Legislative Assembly) represents a constituency at the state level.
questions.solution: MPs sit in the Lok Sabha in Delhi, whereas MLAs sit in the Vidhan Sabha (Legislative Assembly) of their respective states.

questions.question: Why did many countries around the world study India's EVM and VVPAT technology?
questions.answer: India's technological solutions manage the world's largest electorate securely and efficiently, providing a model for other democracies like Namibia and Bhutan.
questions.solution: The robustness and scale of India's electronic voting system have gained international respect and been shared with other nations with support from the ECI.

questions.question: What is the 'Chief Electoral Officer' (CEO) responsible for?
questions.answer: The CEO is responsible for overseeing elections at the State or Union Territory level.
questions.solution: The CEO works under the ECI and coordinates with District Election Officers (DEOs) to manage state-wide polling.

questions.question: Explain how the 'Secret Ballot' ensures a fair election.
questions.answer: A secret ballot ensures that no one else knows who a voter has voted for, protecting the voter from pressure, threats, or intimidation.
questions.solution: As shown in the school case study, booths are set up in corners to ensure privacy, allowing voters to exercise their choice freely and responsively.

questions.question: Interpret Allaidi Krishnaswami Ayyar’s statement that universal franchise 'is a success...never before in the history of the world has such an experiment been so boldly undertaken.'
questions.answer: Ayyar was referring to the fact that no other nation had attempted to give voting rights to such a massive, diverse, and largely illiterate population all at once.
questions.solution: The boldness lay in the 'abundant faith' that the common citizens, regardless of their wealth or education, were wise enough to choose their own leaders.
